content:
# Hydrocarbons

## What is it — Intuitive explanation

Hydrocarbons are **organic compounds made up exclusively of carbon (C) and hydrogen (H)**. Despite their simple composition, the enormous variety of ways carbon atoms can bond together gives rise to a remarkably diverse range of molecules.

Carbon is a special atom with four "hands" (bonding arms). Using these four hands, carbon atoms can link together in chains, form rings, or create double and triple bonds. Much like **combining LEGO bricks**, simply changing the bonding pattern of carbon produces substances that differ completely in their physical state, smell, and reactivity — from gases to liquids to solids.

Hydrocarbons are broadly classified into four types: **alkanes**, which contain only single bonds between carbons; **alkenes**, which contain at least one double bond; **alkynes**, which contain at least one triple bond; and **aromatic compounds**, which contain a benzene ring. Understanding these four categories is the key to unlocking organic chemistry as a whole.

---

## Why it matters — Its place in chemistry

Hydrocarbons are the **starting point** of organic chemistry. Most molecules studied in organic chemistry consist of a hydrocarbon skeleton with oxygen, nitrogen, sulfur, or other elements attached. Understanding the structure and properties of hydrocarbons therefore builds the foundation for understanding all organic compounds, including alcohols, ethers, amines, and carboxylic acids.

Hydrocarbons are also **directly tied to the energy and materials industries**. Petroleum and natural gas are essentially mixtures of hydrocarbons, and gasoline, plastics, synthetic rubber, and pharmaceutical raw materials are all manufactured starting from hydrocarbons. This makes hydrocarbons one of the most tangible connections between chemistry and everyday society.

Furthermore, the classification of hydrocarbons — alkanes, alkenes, alkynes, and aromatics — serves as a **framework for predicting reactivity**. Knowing which type of hydrocarbon you are dealing with immediately gives you a sense of whether the molecule will undergo addition reactions or substitution reactions.

---

## The core ideas — Definitions and key concepts

### Alkanes — Saturated hydrocarbons

Alkanes are hydrocarbons in which all carbon–carbon bonds are **single bonds (C–C)**. Their general formula is:

$$
\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} \quad (n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots)
$$

"Saturated" means the molecule holds the maximum possible number of hydrogen atoms. Representative examples include methane $\text{CH}_4$ ($n=1$), ethane $\text{C}_2\text{H}_6$ ($n=2$), and propane $\text{C}_3\text{H}_8$ ($n=3$). Because they contain only single bonds, electron density is distributed evenly throughout the bonds, making alkanes **relatively unreactive**. Their primary reactions are combustion (oxidation) and, under light irradiation, radical substitution reactions.

### Alkenes — Hydrocarbons with a double bond

Alkenes contain at least one **double bond (C=C)** between carbon atoms. Their general formula is:

$$
\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n} \quad (n = 2, 3, 4, \ldots)
$$

A double bond consists of two types of bonds: a $\sigma$ bond and a $\pi$ bond. The electrons in the $\pi$ bond are held relatively loosely, making the molecule **susceptible to attack by electrophiles**. As a result, alkenes readily undergo **addition reactions**.

### Alkynes — Hydrocarbons with a triple bond

Alkynes contain a **triple bond (C≡C)** between carbon atoms. Their general formula is:

$$
\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n-2} \quad (n = 2, 3, 4, \ldots)
$$

A triple bond consists of one $\sigma$ bond and two $\pi$ bonds. Alkynes are even richer in $\pi$ electrons than alkenes and can undergo addition reactions in two successive steps. The simplest alkyne is acetylene $\text{HC{\equiv}CH}$ (ethyne).

### Aromatic compounds — Cyclic hydrocarbons with special stability

The archetypal aromatic hydrocarbon is benzene, $\text{C}_6\text{H}_6$. Benzene has a cyclic structure in which six carbon atoms are arranged in a regular hexagon, with **six $\pi$ electrons delocalized across the entire ring**.

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_6
$$

This delocalization of electrons gives aromatic compounds exceptional stability. Despite possessing double bonds, benzene does not readily undergo addition reactions the way alkenes do. Instead, **electrophilic aromatic substitution** is its primary reaction pathway. A useful intuition here is that aromatic compounds react in ways that preserve — rather than disrupt — the stability of the ring.

The standard criterion for aromaticity is **Hückel's rule**: a cyclic, planar, conjugated system is aromatic (and thus specially stable) when it contains $4n+2$ $\pi$ electrons ($n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$). Benzene has 6 $\pi$ electrons ($n=1$) and satisfies this condition.

---

## Concrete examples

### Example 1: Propane gas — An everyday alkane

**Propane ($\text{C}_3\text{H}_8$)**, used in portable camping stoves and gas burners, is a classic example of an alkane. Its three carbon atoms are arranged in a straight chain connected entirely by single bonds.

Because alkanes have only single bonds, they are electronically stable and barely react with other substances at room temperature. However, **once ignited, they combust (oxidize) and release a large amount of energy** — which is exactly why propane is used as a fuel. One practical safety note: propane is heavier than air (molecular weight 44), so any leaks tend to accumulate near the floor.

$$
\text{C}_3\text{H}_8 + 5\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 3\text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

This familiar example neatly illustrates the chain of reasoning for alkanes: single bonds only → low reactivity → combustion as the main reaction.

### Example 2: Ethylene — The alkene that ripens fruit

**Ethylene ($\text{H}_2\text{C{=}CH}_2$, ethene)** is the simplest alkene — and remarkably, it is also a **plant hormone** that plants produce naturally to ripen fruit. Have you ever noticed that bananas ripen faster when placed in a bag with other ripe fruit? That is the work of ethylene gas.

From a chemical perspective, the $\text{C{=}C}$ double bond in ethylene serves as the site for addition reactions. For example, adding hydrogen ($\text{H}_2$) across the double bond gives ethane ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_6$), while adding bromine ($\text{Br}_2$) gives 1,2-dibromoethane. Furthermore, ethylene molecules can undergo addition polymerization to form **polyethylene** — the material used in plastic bags and countless plastic products. It is precisely because of the double bond — a "reactive site" — that so many transformations are possible.

### Example 3: Acetylene — The alkyne used in welding and cutting

**Acetylene ($\text{HC{\equiv}CH}$, ethyne)** is the gas used in metal welding and cutting. Its triple bond gives it a combustion temperature of around 3,500°C, hot enough to melt steel with ease.

The abundant $\pi$ electrons in the triple bond make acetylene highly reactive, and it can undergo addition reactions in two stages. The first addition yields a vinyl compound (an alkene); a second addition then gives an alkane. This ability to react in a stepwise manner is extremely valuable in synthetic chemistry. The acid-catalyzed hydration of acetylene to give acetaldehyde was also once an industrially important process.

### Example 4: Benzene and toluene — Familiar faces of aromatic chemistry

**Benzene ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_6$)** is a liquid with a distinctive sweet odor — the very origin of the word "aromatic." Its six carbon atoms form a regular hexagon, with $\pi$ electrons spread uniformly around the entire ring. This arrangement is often visualized as a **"donut-shaped electron cloud"** above and below the ring.

**Toluene (methylbenzene)** is benzene with one methyl group ($-\text{CH}_3$) attached, and is widely used as a solvent in paints and coatings. Aromatic compounds excel at substitution reactions, in which a hydrogen atom on the ring is replaced by another group while the ring's stability is preserved. A well-known example is the nitration of toluene to give trinitrotoluene (TNT), a classic aromatic electrophilic substitution reaction.

---

## Connections — Relationships to other concepts

### Prerequisite knowledge

Understanding hydrocarbons requires a solid grasp of **chemical bonding**. The difference between single, double, and triple bonds comes down to the number of $\sigma$ and $\pi$ bonds present, and this directly determines reactivity. A deeper understanding of **hybrid orbitals** ($sp^3$, $sp^2$, $sp$) further illuminates the three-dimensional structure and reactivity of each type of hydrocarbon. Specifically, carbon in alkanes adopts $sp^3$ hybridization (tetrahedral geometry), carbon in alkene double bonds adopts $sp^2$ hybridization (planar geometry), and carbon in alkyne triple bonds adopts $sp$ hybridization (linear geometry).

### Position within organic chemistry

Hydrocarbons form the **backbone** of organic chemistry. Everything you study afterward builds on this foundation:

- **Alcohols and ethers**: hydrocarbon skeletons with $-\text{OH}$ or $-\text{O}-$ groups added
- **Carboxylic acids and esters**: compounds containing $-\text{COOH}$ or $-\text{COO}-$
- **Amines and amides**: nitrogen-containing organic compounds

All of these can be understood as "a hydrocarbon skeleton with a functional group attached." Because the type of hydrocarbon framework — alkane-type, alkene-type, or aromatic — influences the reactivity of the functional groups attached to it, knowing hydrocarbon classification is equivalent to having a map of all of organic chemistry.

### Connection to stereochemistry

Because rotation around a double bond is restricted in alkenes, **cis-trans isomerism (geometric isomerism)** can arise. Two isomers with the same molecular formula but different spatial arrangements of groups can exist. This difference in geometry can have a profound impact on biological activity — for instance in pharmaceuticals — and leads naturally into the more advanced topic of stereochemistry.

---

## Applications in other fields

### Petrochemistry and the energy industry

Crude oil is primarily a mixture of various hydrocarbons. Refining separates it into gasoline (a mixture of $\text{C}_5$–$\text{C}_{10}$ alkanes), kerosene, diesel fuel, heavy oil, and other fractions. Alkenes and alkynes generated during refining are further processed into **polymeric materials** — plastics, synthetic rubber, and synthetic fibers. It is no exaggeration to say that modern industrial society is built on hydrocarbon chemistry.

### Pharmaceuticals and biochemistry

Many drugs and biomolecules contain aromatic rings as their core structural framework. Aspirin, ibuprofen, antibiotics, and steroid hormones all feature benzene rings or their derivatives as central structural elements in medicinal chemistry. The DNA bases — adenine, guanine, cytosine, and thymine — also possess aromatic character and play essential roles in biological information transfer.

### Materials science and polymer chemistry

Polyethylene from addition polymerization of ethylene (an alkene), polypropylene from propylene, and conducting polymers such as polyacetylene derived from acetylenic hydrocarbons — hydrocarbons are the fundamental raw materials for **advanced materials development**. The discovery of polyacetylene and the elucidation of its electrical conductivity earned the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2000, demonstrating that hydrocarbon chemistry is deeply connected to cutting-edge materials science.

### Environmental science

**Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs)**, generated by incomplete combustion from vehicles and industrial facilities, are air pollutants closely linked to environmental and public health concerns. Compounds such as benzo[a]pyrene are known for their carcinogenicity, and a solid understanding of the chemical properties of hydrocarbons underpins the environmental monitoring techniques and mitigation technologies used to address these issues.

terms.definition: A general term for organic compounds consisting only of carbon (C) and hydrogen (H). They are broadly classified into alkanes, alkenes, alkynes, and aromatic compounds.

A saturated hydrocarbon in which all carbon atoms are connected by single bonds (C–C), represented by the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. Alkanes have low reactivity and primarily undergo combustion reactions.
A hydrocarbon containing at least one carbon–carbon double bond (C＝C), represented by the general formula CₙH₂ₙ. The presence of a π bond makes alkenes prone to addition reactions.

A hydrocarbon containing a carbon–carbon triple bond (C≡C), represented by the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₋₂. Rich in π electrons, alkynes are highly reactive and can undergo addition reactions in two stages.

A compound with special stability arising from the delocalization of π electrons in a cyclic structure such as a benzene ring. Electrophilic aromatic substitution, rather than addition reactions, is the primary reaction pathway.
A covalent bond formed by the direct, head-on overlap of two atomic orbitals along the bond axis. Sigma bonds are present in all single, double, and triple bonds.

A covalent bond formed by the lateral (side-on) overlap of two atomic orbitals with respect to the bond axis. Pi bonds are found in double and triple bonds and are susceptible to attack by electrophiles.
A reaction in which atoms or groups of atoms are added across a double or triple bond. It is a characteristic reaction type for alkenes and alkynes.
A reaction in which a hydrogen atom on the ring of an aromatic compound is replaced by another group via an electrophile. The reaction proceeds while preserving the stability of the aromatic ring.

A state in which electrons are not confined to a specific bond but are spread over a wide region within the molecule. The delocalization of π electrons in benzene is the source of the stability of aromatic compounds.

A new orbital formed by the mixing of atomic orbitals. The sp³, sp², and sp hybridizations of carbon explain the differences in three-dimensional structure and reactivity among alkanes, alkenes, and alkynes.
A phenomenon in which two isomers with the same molecular formula but different spatial arrangements exist due to restricted rotation around a double bond. It is also called geometric isomerism.
A reaction in which monomers bearing double bonds, such as alkenes, undergo successive addition reactions to form a polymer. The formation of polyethylene from ethylene is a representative example.

A general term for aromatic hydrocarbons with structures consisting of multiple fused benzene rings. They are produced by incomplete combustion and are associated with environmental and health concerns such as carcinogenicity.

diagrams.name: concept-diagram.svg
diagrams.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 600 520" width="600" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" font-family="system-ui, sans-serif">

  <!-- Center: Hydrocarbon -->
  <ellipse cx="300" cy="260" rx="72" ry="36" fill="#3b82f6" fill-opacity="0.15" stroke="#3b82f6" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="300" y="255" text-anchor="middle" font-size="15" font-weight="bold" fill="#3b82f6">Hydrocarbon</text>
  <text x="300" y="272" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#3b82f6">C and H only</text>

  <!-- Top: Alkane -->
  <rect x="60" y="30" width="140" height="90" rx="12" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.07" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="130" y="56" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" font-weight="bold" fill="currentColor">Alkane</text>
  <text x="130" y="74" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="currentColor">C–C single bonds only</text>
  <text x="130" y="90" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#3b82f6">CₙH₂ₙ₊₂</text>
  <text x="130" y="108" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">e.g. Methane CH₄</text>

  <!-- Top right: Alkene -->
  <rect x="400" y="30" width="140" height="90" rx="12" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.07" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="470" y="56" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" font-weight="bold" fill="currentColor">Alkene</text>
  <text x="470" y="74" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="currentColor">Contains C＝C double bond</text>
  <text x="470" y="90" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#3b82f6">CₙH₂ₙ</text>
  <text x="470" y="108" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">e.g. Ethylene C₂H₄</text>

  <!-- Bottom left: Alkyne -->
  <rect x="60" y="390" width="140" height="90" rx="12" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.07" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="130" y="416" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" font-weight="bold" fill="currentColor">Alkyne</text>
  <text x="130" y="434" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="currentColor">Contains C≡C triple bond</text>
  <text x="130" y="450" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#3b82f6">CₙH₂ₙ₋₂</text>
  <text x="130" y="468" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">e.g. Acetylene C₂H₂</text>

  <!-- Bottom right: Aromatic -->
  <rect x="400" y="390" width="140" height="90" rx="12" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.07" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="470" y="416" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" font-weight="bold" fill="currentColor">Aromatic</text>
  <text x="470" y="434" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="currentColor">Benzene ring · delocalized π</text>
  <text x="470" y="450" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#3b82f6">C₆H₆ (Benzene)</text>
  <text x="470" y="468" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">e.g. Benzene, Toluene</text>

  <!-- Connecting line: Center → Alkane -->
  <line x1="240" y1="243" x2="190" y2="115" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="5,3" marker-end="url(#arr)"/>
  <!-- Connecting line: Center → Alkene -->
  <line x1="360" y1="243" x2="410" y2="115" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="5,3" marker-end="url(#arr)"/>
  <!-- Connecting line: Center → Alkyne -->
  <line x1="240" y1="277" x2="190" y2="395" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="5,3" marker-end="url(#arr)"/>
  <!-- Connecting line: Center → Aromatic -->
  <line x1="360" y1="277" x2="410" y2="395" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="5,3" marker-end="url(#arr)"/>

  <!-- Arrow markers -->
  <defs>
    <marker id="arr" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="8" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z" fill="currentColor"/>
    </marker>
    <marker id="arr2" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="8" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z" fill="#22c55e"/>
    </marker>
    <marker id="arr3" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="8" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z" fill="#ef4444"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>

  <!-- Reactivity axis label -->
  <!-- Reactivity arrow (left vertical) -->
  <line x1="22" y1="430" x2="22" y2="80" stroke="#ef4444" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#arr3)"/>
  <text x="14" y="270" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#ef4444" transform="rotate(-90,14,270)">Reactivity (low → high)</text>

  <!-- Unsaturation arrow (top horizontal) -->
  <line x1="210" y1="15" x2="390" y2="15" stroke="#22c55e" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#arr2)"/>
  <text x="300" y="11" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#22c55e">Degree of unsaturation (low → high)</text>

  <!-- Bond type comparison line (Alkane→Alkene→Alkyne) -->
  <!-- σ only → σ+π → σ+2π -->
  <!-- Label: Bond type -->
  <text x="300" y="198" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="currentColor" opacity="0.7">Bond type</text>
  <text x="130" y="158" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">σ only</text>
  <text x="470" y="158" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">σ + π</text>
  <text x="130" y="368" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">σ + 2π</text>
  <text x="470" y="368" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">delocalized π</text>

  <!-- Main reaction labels -->
  <text x="50" y="168" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#ef4444">Combustion·</text>
  <text x="50" y="180" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#ef4444">Substitution</text>
  <text x="548" y="168" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#3b82f6">Addition reaction</text>
  <text x="50" y="358" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#3b82f6">Addition reaction</text>
  <text x="548" y="356" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#22c55e">Electrophilic</text>
  <text x="548" y="368" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#22c55e">substitution</text>

  <!-- Organic compound foundation label -->
  <rect x="195" y="290" width="210" height="34" rx="8" fill="#3b82f6" fill-opacity="0.1" stroke="#3b82f6" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="300" y="309" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#3b82f6">Backbone of organic chemistry</text>
  <text x="300" y="323" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#3b82f6">(foundation for other functional groups)</text>

  <!-- Benzene ring schematic (small) -->
  <g transform="translate(448,445)">
    <polygon points="0,-12 10.4,-6 10.4,6 0,12 -10.4,6 -10.4,-6" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.2" opacity="0.5"/>
    <circle cx="0" cy="0" r="6.5" fill="none" stroke="#3b82f6" stroke-width="1" opacity="0.7"/>
  </g>

</svg>
